So, everybody's tragedy is somebody's opportunity. So, all these practices were insurance driven, right? Which the lazy doctors were all dependent on, but there's their list, okay? If you ask them for their list, they all had the same list, okay? We hate the insurance companies, we hate the paperwork, we hate them rejecting cases, we hate them coming back and saying, you want to bill us $56, we're only going to give you $18, they hate the caps, yada, yada, yada. This is a whole list, okay? That's it. This is a whole list, okay? So, what's the replacement? Cash. Exactly, okay? The cash practice, which is what we called it. And so our replacement was no insurance. We're going to show you how to run a successful practice and make all the money you want, and you accept no insurance, none zero, all right? If somebody wants to go get reimbursed on their own and fight with their insurance company, that's fine, but everybody's going to give you cash. Furthermore, since we're going to get cash from them anyway, let's get it in one big lump sum, so let's sell prepay. And so we taught prepay. So now, if you've been to a chiropractor, you know the drill, right? You need to come in five times a week for the first six more of a few. So all that gets calculated up, and no, it's a $13,800 case, and you can take care of it in one payment or three payment, and you save 20% because now you can give them discounts, which you can't do if you part cash, part insurance, because you can't legally do anything for the cash patient. You don't do for the insurance patient, but if you're not taking any insurance, you can do any damn thing you want. Yeah, you can save 20%. If you do one payment, you save 10% if you do three, right? So that was all replacement. Now, I'll give you another example, same profession and dentists. So Greg Stanley, who some of you may know, very successful, practiced consultant for many years. He hit a wall in his business because some people have this problem. Great brand name, great reputation, but everybody in the niche thought they knew what he was about. So if you stopped everybody at a trade show, chiropractor or dentist, and you said, what do you know about Greg Stanley? Almost without exception, they would start by saying good things. Good guy, good reputation, does what he says he's going to do, et cetera. And then they would tell you his program on a matchbook cover. He tells us to get our overhead down below 50%, pay off all our credit cards, live debt free, invest in municipal bonds, and put the staff on his program. That's his whole deal. Well, so who's coming to a seminar? Who's buying a program? There's no mystery why he hit the wall. I said, you got to keep the reputation and scrap the content, right? We need a replacement. So chiropractors and dentists, now instead of insurance, if you ask them to make another list about all their pains, the thing that aggravates them the most, that they're least happy with, what do you think's on the list? Number one on the list. Staff, absolutely. Number one now they're going to tell you, because none of it's their fault, of course. Nothing going on in the office is their responsibility. So it's entirely the staff, right? And one of the big gripes about the staff is they never refer. Now, by the way, if you start to talk to doctors about referrals and why their referral rate's so shitty, which it is, they will immediately tell you, well, I got 18 staff people in here, and they don't even bring anybody in from their family. And they don't encourage the patients to refer, and so, you know, we run a referral promotion, and we put the little button on them, like the Herbalife button, you know, lose weight, ask me how, you know, so we put the button on them, and, you know, nothing happens, right? So, okay, what's the replacement? We call it staff-driven practice. Here's the old pitch. Stop, forget advertising, forget marketing, don't spend any more money, which they don't want to spend that anyway, right? So I love that deal, right? Stop it all. Don't be in Valpak. Don't do nothing, right? Send your staff to us for three days, and we'll return your staff to you as a referral machine, right? And they will drag people in off the street. They will bring their family in. They will talk to the patients for you. They will get the patients to refer, and your practice will increase by 10% a month every month, so it'll double every year, purely based on the efforts of the staff. It replaces all practice. By the way, it's not an easy promise to keep. But as I told Greg, that ain't my job. My job's coming up with a replacement, right? Making the thing work is entirely your damn problem. I don't get paid enough to solve that, too. So those are replacement examples in which you really don't take the person out of the business, right? But you literally keep the name of the business, and in his mind, change the entire business, okay? The second thing that might be in this drawer would be true replacement. So like, there's a very successful thing being run in chiropractic right now, and the guy, literally, the ad in the trade journal is all about getting out of practice, okay? The guy is teaching commodities trading to chiropractors. The whole pitch is a week's pay and four hours, and never see a patient again as long as you live, unless you want to, alright? Now, I'm actually surprised the trade journal took it. Usually you have to do those kind of things by direct mail, because the trade journals won't run the ads. But right now, trade journals, magazines are taking ads they wouldn't take two years ago, because they're a little short on advertisers. So he actually is doing a pure greener pastures pitch. And if he hit his stuff, the whole opening is everything that's horrible about being in practice, and everything that's going to be horrible about being in practice, and why I got out. And, you know, I started doing this, and 12 months later, I was able to burn my practices to the ground and walk away. And we actually used a similar pitch for Get Rich in Real Estate for Docs with Ben Altadonna program a few years ago. We didn't quite go that far, but our star was a guy who was up to eight offices, and they were driving him crazy, and he was one step away from his order blowing out from the stress, and he started to do real estate. And then three years later, he was able to sell his practices, and now he just gets a check in the mail and sits on his boat. So it was close to the same story.
